---
title: "Savannah Milkweed (Asclepias pedicellata)"
slug: "asclepias-pedicellata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474779170/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Savannah Milkweed"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Poaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Poaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474779170/medium.jpg" alt="Savannah Milkweed" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/marktancig">
marktancig</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264338670">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Poaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=158754)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Uncommon/rare species in Florida found in dry-to-wet pine savannas and flatwoods.

## Natural history notes

A slender wildflower characterized by its very narrow, grass-like leaves and small white to greenish-white flowers. In Florida, it is found in dry, sandy habitats such as flatwoods, sandhills, and open pine woodlands. It typically blooms during the warmer months and serves as a host plant for monarch and other milkweed butterflies, as well as a nectar source for pollinators.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/158754-Asclepias-pedicellata)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-asclepias-pedicellata/)
